Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Why the race to build the ultimate NVIDIA Killer chip is dead, and how Silicon Valley realized it was fighting the wrong war all along.
For the past three years, Silicon Valley has been locked in a multi-billion-dollar delusion.
The narrative was simple: NVIDIA’s astronomical $3+ trillion valuation was a temporary monopoly built on a chip shortage. Every tech giant from Google, Amazon, and Meta to ambitious semiconductor startups like Groq, Cerebras, and Sambanova rushed to build the ultimate NVIDIA Killer. AMD launched its powerhouse MI300X, Intel bet big on Gaudi, and venture capitalists poured tens of billions into alternative AI silicon.
The consensus among VCs was clear: Compute is a commodity. Eventually, hardware supply will catch up, prices will drop, and NVIDIA’s moat will evaporate.
They were wrong.
As we cross into mid-2026, the dust from the AI hardware wars has finally settled, and the verdict is absolute: The war is over. NVIDIA didn’t just win; they rewrote the rules of engagement so profoundly that the resistance didn’t even realize they were playing the wrong game.
Here is how NVIDIA’s masterclass in architectural engineering and ecosystem…
